keyMyths.title: The Pharaoh's Heresy
keyMyths.description: Akhenaten, pharaoh of Egypt, breaks with tradition and establishes Atenism, a monotheistic religion centered on the sun god Aten. This act of religious revolution shakes the foundations of Egyptian society and sets the stage for later events.
keyMyths.source: The Great Hymn to the Aten

keyMyths.title: The Exodus Narrative
keyMyths.description: Moses, raised in the Egyptian court, witnesses the suffering of the Israelites and is called by Yahweh to lead them out of slavery. He confronts Pharaoh, performs miraculous feats, and ultimately leads the Israelites through the Red Sea to freedom.

keyMyths.title: The Revelation at Sinai
keyMyths.description: After escaping Egypt, Moses leads the Israelites to Mount Sinai, where he receives the Ten Commandments and the Torah from Yahweh. This covenant establishes the foundation of Jewish law and tradition.

keyMyths.title: The Golden Calf
keyMyths.description: While Moses is on Mount Sinai, the Israelites, fearing his absence, create a golden calf to worship. This act of idolatry angers Moses and Yahweh, leading to a period of repentance and reaffirmation of the covenant.

keyMyths.title: Conquest of Canaan
keyMyths.description: Following Moses' death, Joshua leads the Israelites in their conquest of the land of Canaan, promised to them by Yahweh. This involves numerous battles and the establishment of the Israelite kingdom.

achievements: Challenging the established polytheistic religion of Egypt
Leading the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt
Receiving the Ten Commandments and the Torah at Mount Sinai
Performing miraculous feats to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelites
Establishing a monotheistic covenant with Yahweh
Guiding the Israelites through the wilderness for forty years
Conquering the land of Canaan and establishing the Israelite kingdom

sources.name: The Hebrew Bible (Tanakh)
sources.description: The primary source for Jewish mythology and history, including the books of Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy, and Joshua.
sources.title: The Hebrew Bible (Tanakh)

sources.name: The Great Hymn to the Aten
sources.description: An ancient Egyptian text praising the sun god Aten, central to Akhenaten's religious reforms.
sources.title: The Great Hymn to the Aten

sources.name: Midrash
sources.description: A collection of rabbinic interpretations and expansions on the Hebrew Bible, providing additional context and stories.
sources.title: Midrash

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews
sources.description: A first-century CE historical account of the Jewish people by the Roman-Jewish historian Josephus.
sources.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews

sources.name: Assmann, Jan. Moses the Egyptian: The Memory of Egypt in Western Monotheism.
sources.description: An academic exploration of the influence of Egyptian religion on the development of monotheism.
sources.title: Assmann, Jan. Moses the Egyptian: The Memory of Egypt in Western Monotheism.

sources.name: Redford, Donald B. Akhenaten: The Heretic King.
sources.description: A scholarly biography of Akhenaten and his religious revolution.
sources.title: Redford, Donald B. Akhenaten: The Heretic King.
